coredata.dc:title: Iron ions-sequestrable and antioxidative carbon dot-based nano-formulation with nitric oxide release for Parkinson's disease treatment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Wei
Ji, Min
Li, Yingjie
Qian, Min
Qin, Yanhui
Li, Wenshuai
Nie, Huifang
Lv, Wenxin
Jiang, Guangwei
Huang, Rong
Lin, Chenteng
Li, Hongyuan
Huang, Rongqin
coredata.dc:description: Nondestructive penetration of the blood-brain barrier (BBB) to specifically prevent iron deposition and the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) shows great potential for treating Parkinson's disease (PD). However, effective agents with distinct mechanisms of action remain scarce. Herein, a N-doping carbon dot (CD) emitting red light was prepared, which can sacrifice ROS and produce nitric oxide (NO) owing to its surface N-involved groups conjugated to the sp2-hybrided π-system. Meanwhile, CD can chelate iron ions, thus depressing the catalytic Fe cycle and *OH detaching to inhibit the Fenton reaction. By modifying lactoferrin (Lf) via polyethylene glycol (PEG), the resulting CD-PEG-Lf (CPL) can nondestructively cross the BBB, targeting the dopaminergic neurons via both NO-mediated reversible BBB opening and Lf receptor-mediated transportation. Accordingly, it can serve as an antioxidant, reducing oxidative stress via its unique iron chelation, free radical sacrificing, and synergy with iron reflux prevention originating from Lf. Thus, it can significantly reduce brain inflammation and improve the behavioral performance of PD mice. Additionally, CPL can image the PD via its red fluorescence. Finally, this platform can be metabolized out of the brain through cerebrospinal fluid circulation without causing obvious side effects, promising a robust treatment for PD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dot
Parkinson's disease
Blood-brain barrier
Antiinflammation
Iron sequestration

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Innovation Plan Laboratory Animal Research Project of Shanghai
National Key R&D Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (82172746), National Key R&D Program of China, MOST (2023YFC2510000) and Science and Technology Innovation Plan Laboratory Animal Research Project of Shanghai (23141900400).

To understand these unique performances of CD (Fig. 2A), further characterizations and DFT calculations were provided. First, Fe2+ adsorption onto CD broadened and shifted left the stretching vibration of O\u2013H/N\u2013H groups at around 3300\u20133500 cm\u22121 (Fig. 2B), implying the increased force constant of the N\u2013H/O\u2013H bonds. This could be attributed to the electronic cloud shifting from H towards O/N upon coordination of Fe2+ with the CD, which was also supported by calculating the charge distribution (Fig. S5). Meanwhile, the C[dbnd]N stretching vibration at 1520 cm\u22121, which arised from the conjugation between benzene ring and nitrogen with lone pair electrons (pyridinic nitrogen), was weakened, while the stretching vibration peak of Fe\u2013N appears at 400-600 cm\u22121 after Fe2+ adsorption [23,24]. These suggested the Fe2+ adsorption onto CD via the possible Fe\u2013N chelation on pyridinic nitrogen of the framework. This Fe\u2013N chelation brought the Fe2+ with high and irreversible oxidation-reduction potential, which thus could not catalyze the Fenton reaction due to the blocked reaction path (Fig. 2C). Investigation of ROS scavenging found that the surface groups of CD such as aminos contributed the reducing power. After reaction with ROS, these surface groups, especially the amino-N contained groups were obvious decreased as indicated by NMR and XPS (Fig. 2D and Fig. S6), which suggested their oxidation removal and conversion (such as conversion into C[dbnd]N species) after scavenging the ROS. This ROS scavenging destroyed the surface states and thus caused a decreased fluorescence emission especially under the exorbitant ROS (H2O2) stimulation (Herein, this didn't affect the fluorescence imaging of CD due to relatively low H2O2 concentration of about 100 \u03BCM) (Fig. 2E and Fig. S7). Moreover, the loss of surface N-contained groups enabled CD to profile a concentration-dependent NO release especially in the presence of oxidative microenvironment (Fig. 2F). And this process could be slightly inhibited after GSH addition. Thus, the NO release might be attributed to the nitrogen doping-introduced amino onto CD, enabling CD to release NO via oxidation reaction. These suggested the unique structure of CD for Fe2+ sequestration, ROS scavenging and NO release.This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (82172746), National Key R&D Program of China, MOST (2023YFC2510000) and Science and Technology Innovation Plan Laboratory Animal Research Project of Shanghai (23141900400).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of CD. (A) TEM image, (B) HRTEM image and (C) corresponding FFT pattern of CD. The inset in (A) is the size distribution of CD, and the yellow dot line in (B) indicates the lattice fringes. (D) Schematic diagram for the structure of CD. (E) XPS survey and high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra (inset) of CD. (F) UV–Vis absorption (Abs), PL excitation (Ex) and emission spectra (Em) of CD. (G) Iron concentrations chelated on different concentrations of CD assessed by ICP. The grey histograms showed the initial Fe concentrations in solution. (H) Inhibition rates for different concentrations of CD on Fenton reaction assessed by salicylic acid method (n = 3). (I) ESR spectra for the Fenton reaction solution in presence of CD or not. (J) Scavenging of DPPH free radicals by reaction with CD at different concentrations for varied time periods. (K) NO generations of CD with different concentrations incubated in 100 μM H2O2-contained solution for 60 min. The inset is NO generations of 500 μg/mL CD incubated in 100 μM H2O2-contained solution for different time periods. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Exploration for the mechanisms of multiple activities of CD. (A) Diagrams for iron chelation reaction and ROS scavenging associated with NO generation reaction of CD. (B) FT-IR spectra and (C) cyclic voltammetry curves of CD before and after iron ions chelation. The inset in (c) is the CV curves of K3Fe(CN)6. (D) 1H NMR of CD before and after oxidation by H2O2. (E) Fluorescence spectra of CD after incubation with different concentrations of H2O2 for 24 h. (F) NO generations of CD under different solutions. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3).

DFT calculations for the mechanism exploration. (A–C) Calculated electrostatic potential maps and corresponding HOMOs/LUMOs for g-CD, n-CD and as prepared CD, respectively. (D) Mechanism of CD in inhibiting Fenton reaction. (E) Gibbs free energy diagram for Fenton reaction with free Fe2+ or CD-chelated Fe2+ (CD-Fe2+). (F) HOMOs and LUMOs of free Fe3+, Fe3+ chelation on n-CD (n-CD-Fe3+), and Fe3+ chelation on as prepared CD (CD-Fe3+), respectively. (G) Gibbs free energy diagram and (H) reaction process for CD reacted with H2O2.

The modification of Lf optimized the bio-application of CD in vitro and in vivo. (A) TEM and (B) magnified TEM images of CPL. The inset in (A) is FFT pattern of CPL. The white dot line in (B) circles individual CPL nanoparticle while the yellow dot lines circle the inside CD. The inset in (B) is HRTEM image of the inside CD, where the lines label the lattice fringes. (C) Excitation-emission maps and (D) ζ-potentials of different formulations. The inset in (D) are photographs of different formulations under sunlight and ultraviolet light irradiations, respectively. (E) Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images and (F) corresponding fluorescence quantifications of LO2 normal hepatocytes, SH-SY5Y human neuroblastoma cells and bEnd.3 vascular endothelial cells incubated with different formulation for 60 min. Scale bar = 200 μm. (G) Representative fluorescence images at different time points after intravenous administration of different formulations in MPTP model mice or in healthy mice. (H) The quantitative fluorescence intensity in the brain imaging of MPTP model mice and healthy mice at different time points after intravenous injection of CPL. (I) Ex vivo images of major organs from the mice at 8 h after treatment according to (G). Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Cellular level exploration of CD-based formulations for PD treatment. (A) Experimental schemes for determining the efflux behavior of CPL. (B) Extracellular fluorescence (red line, n = 8) of solutions and intracellular fluorescence (black line, n = 3) after different incubation time periods. (C) Iron contents in the final supernatant after 240 min incubation measured by ICP-MS (n = 3). (D) CLSM images of cells at different time periods after the replacement of CPL. Scale bar = 50 μm. (E) CLSM images showing intracellular ROS detection in MPP+-induced SH-SY5Y cells after 24-h incubation with various formulations. The SH-SY5Y cells without any treatment were used as controls. Green: DCF fluorescence produced from the oxidization of nonfluorescent DCFH by ROS. Scale bar = 100 μm. (F) Fluorescence intensities determining the LIP levels within SH-SY5Y cells after 30-min incubation with different agents (n = 3). (G) Cell viability of MPP+-induced SH-SY5Y cells after treatment with different agents (n = 6). (H) Western blot (WB) images and corresponding quantifications for DMT1 protein expression levels in MPP+-induced SH-SY5Y cells after treatment by different formulations (n = 3). The SH-SY5Y cells without any treatment were used as controls. (I) Flow cytometry analysis of NO generations in bEnd.3 cells after treatment by different concentrations of CD (n = 3). The NO was detected by DAF-FM fluorescence probe. (J) WB images of Claudin-5, MMP-9 and ZO-1 expressions in bEnd.3 cells after different treatments (Drug induced) or withdrawing these treatments (Withdraw recovery). (K) The in vitro BBB permeabilities and transport ratios of different formulations at various incubation time periods. Data are presented as mean ± SD. ****p < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Behavioral evaluation of PD mice treated with different formulations. (A) Timeline for constructing an MPTP-induced PD mouse model and administering various formulations, with untreated healthy mice serving as controls. (B) Rotarod behaviors of MPTP-induced PD mice following different treatments (n = 6). (C) Trajectory images of open field tests for MPTP-induced PD mice after different treatments. (D)Total travelled distance, (E) average speed and (F) central motion ratio (middle/all) in the open field tests. Data are presented as mean ± SD. (n = 8). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

Pathological changes of PD mice treated with different formulations. (A) Immunofluorescence images for TH (green) in the striatum and SN, with representative images displayed at the same bregma position. Scale bar = 200 μm. (B) DA levels in SN. (n = 4). (C) Immunohistochemistry staining for iron (arrows) in SN. Scale bar = 200 μm. (D) Total iron content inside the brain. (E) CLSM images for M1-phenotype microglia (indication by the CD86 expression, green) and M2-phenotype microglia (indication by the CD206 expression) in the SN of brain slices (scale bar = 200 μm), and (F–I) MDA, IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α levels in brain tissues, derived from MPTP-induced mice after different treatments. Healthy mice without any treatments were used as controls. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 6). **p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Schematic illustration for the synthesis and multifunctional characteristics of CD-based formulations. (A) Schematic illustrations for the synthesis of different nano-formulations. (B) Multiple functions of CPL. (C) Illustration of iron ions-sequestrable and antioxidative CD-based nano-formulation with NO release for PD treatment.

head.ce:title: Iron ions-sequestrable and antioxidative carbon dot-based nano-formulation with nitric oxide release for Parkinson's disease treatment

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials
College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Advanced Low-Dimension Materials, State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Donghua University, Shanghai, 201600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nondestructive penetration of the blood-brain barrier (BBB) to specifically prevent iron deposition and the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) shows great potential for treating Parkinson's disease (PD). However, effective agents with distinct mechanisms of action remain scarce. Herein, a N-doping carbon dot (CD) emitting red light was prepared, which can sacrifice ROS and produce nitric oxide (NO) owing to its surface N-involved groups conjugated to the sp2-hybrided π-system. Meanwhile, CD can chelate iron ions, thus depressing the catalytic Fe cycle and *OH detaching to inhibit the Fenton reaction. By modifying lactoferrin (Lf) via polyethylene glycol (PEG), the resulting CD-PEG-Lf (CPL) can nondestructively cross the BBB, targeting the dopaminergic neurons via both NO-mediated reversible BBB opening and Lf receptor-mediated transportation. Accordingly, it can serve as an antioxidant, reducing oxidative stress via its unique iron chelation, free radical sacrificing, and synergy with iron reflux prevention originating from Lf. Thus, it can significantly reduce brain inflammation and improve the behavioral performance of PD mice. Additionally, CPL can image the PD via its red fluorescence. Finally, this platform can be metabolized out of the brain through cerebrospinal fluid circulation without causing obvious side effects, promising a robust treatment for PD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the incidence rapidly increases, Parkinson's disease (PD) greatly affects human living quality due to its serious symptoms including resting tremors, bradykinesia and muscle rigidity [1]. Current treatment for PD is dopamine replacement therapy. However, owing to the obstruction of blood-brain barrier (BBB) and the side-effects of usually overdosed dopamine in non-focal area, this therapy is often far from expectations [2]. What's worse, this dopamine replacement therapy can only relieve symptoms but cannot prevent disease progression because of its inability to treat the cause of PD [3]. Therefore, exploring new drugs or therapies that can cross the BBB to stop or eliminate the cause of PD remains an urgent challenge.

As known, reactive oxygen species (ROS) are considered one cause of PD, which would promote the inflammation and lead to the death of dopamine neurons [4]. Therefore, various ROS scavengers such as vitamin C and Coenzyme Q10 have been widely investigated their therapeutic potential for the PD treatment [5,6]. However, only ROS scavenging has not been explored enough since iron deposition-induced Fenton reaction can continuously supplement the ROS [7]. Moreover, iron deposition would also cause the formation of alpha-synuclein fibers and induce the ferroptosis of dopamine neurons [8]. Accordingly, iron chelate treatment strategy has been flourished in recent years, proven to be more effective in previous studies [9,10]. Nevertheless, current iron chelating agents, such as deferoxamine (DFO), have poor bioavailability, limited penetration through the BBB, particularly non-targeted iron chelation may cause the risk of systemic side effects [11]. What is more, the back-flow into the neurocytes of the chelated iron would result in the failure of treatment. Meanwhile, ROS generation beyond Fenton reaction of iron deposition could not be eliminated by the traditional iron chelating agents. Therefore, preparation of novel iron chelating agents that can cross the BBB, resist the iron reflux and systematically eliminate the ROS, is of great demand.

Carbon dot (CD) has recently shown great potential in bioimaging and theranostics due to its excellent biocompatibility, good photostability and various biological effects [12,13]. For example, CD has been used as an antioxidant to scavenge ROS and prevent oxidative stress-induced cell death [14,15]. By special design and synthesis, some CD could emit red fluorescence, facilitating the deeply penetrated bioimaging [16]. Moreover, CD has been reported to possess special interactions with some metal ions, which could be exploited as a biosensor [17]. Particularly, our previous work found that the N-doping CD could cross the BBB for glioma treatment [18]. All these suggest the potential of CD in brain disease treatments. Nevertheless, red-emitting CD that can synergize the multifunction of nondestructive BBB crossing, iron reflux prevention and systematical ROS elimination for PD treatment has seldom been exploited. Especially, investigations into the mechanisms of these functions, which were often neglected in previous studies, are of great importance for the practical applications.

Therefore, this work developed a facile solvothermal reaction to prepare red-emitting N-doping CD. This CD can not only sacrifice ROS for antioxidation and simultaneously produce nitric oxide (NO) for reversible opening of the BBB, but also chelate iron ions to inhibit the Fenton reaction. Meanwhile, in considering that lactoferrin (Lf) receptor is widely over-expressed on vascular endothelial cells and substantia nigra (SN) dopaminergic neurons in PD cases and MPTP-intoxicated mice [19], Lf was used to graft this CD, obtaining a Lf-modified carbon dot (CPL). The as-synthesized CPL furtherly elevated the BBB permeability via Lf receptor-mediated transportation and selectively accumulated into the SN dopaminergic neurons through receptor-mediated pathway. More importantly, it was found that the modified Lf could reduce the expression of divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1), through which the reflux of Fe2+ into the cell was inhibited. Subsequently, a synergistical strategy of the BBB crossing, PD targeting, iron sequestration and ROS sacrificing, was achieved, which could improve the behavioral performance of PD mice in MPTP models in terms of effectively protecting SN dopamine neurons and reducing the oxidative stress in the brain, without other side effects. Additionally, this CPL could image the PD via its red fluorescence. And it was also found CPL could be metabolized out of the brain via the cerebrospinal fluid circulation. Correspondingly, all these functions were mechanistically explained via various characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) calculation, which promise a robust therapeutic agent for PD.

CD was synthesized via a simple solvothermal approach from p-phenylenediamines (Scheme 1 ), which exhibited a uniformed size around 4.5 nm (Fig. 1 A and B) and an average molecular weight of about 9546 Da (Fig. S1). This CD had a graphitic carbon core as observed from the well-resolved lattice fringes of typical (100) plane in HRTEM image and correspondingly the dot-ring like diffraction in ED pattern (Fig. 1C). Meanwhile, this CD had plentiful N and O-contained surface groups such as the possible amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl, as observed from XPS (Fig. 1E) and FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 B). Particularly, some N species were doped into the framework of CD to form pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen [20], as revealed by the deconvoluted high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 1E). Accordingly, the structure of CD was speculated as Fig. 1D. This CD had distinct UV–Vis absorptions (Fig. 1F) originated from the π → π* transitions of CC bonds in aromatic sp2 domains, and the n → π* transitions of CO/CN bonds in surface functional groups [21]. Meanwhile, the sp2-hybrided domains coupled with the surface functional groups produced an extra absorption around 500 nm, corresponding to the fluorescence excitation spectrum (Fig. 1F). Thus, this CD had exciting-light wavelength-independent red fluorescence emission (Fig. S2), which was different from previously reported CD with size (wavelength)-dependent emissions [22]. Interestingly, it was found that this CD can adsorb divalent iron ions (Fe2+) (Figs. S3 and 4). And at a CD concentration of 100 μg/mL and a Fe2+ concentration of 28.85 μg/mL, the adsorption amount could reach 21.90 μg/mL, achieving almost 75.91 % Fe2+ removal (Fig. 1G). As known, Fe2+ could catalyze Fenton reaction to produce radicals. However, this effect was remarkably inhibited via uniformly mixing Fe2+ with CD (Fig. 1H and I), which suggested the lost catalytic activity of Fe2+ after adsorption onto CD. Besides that, CD also possesses the ability to scavenge free radicals and generate NO, both which exhibited a dose-dependent manner and a time-dependent characteristics (Fig. 1J and K).

To understand these unique performances of CD (Fig. 2A), further characterizations and DFT calculations were provided. First, Fe2+ adsorption onto CD broadened and shifted left the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H groups at around 3300–3500 cm−1 (Fig. 2B), implying the increased force constant of the N–H/O–H bonds. This could be attributed to the electronic cloud shifting from H towards O/N upon coordination of Fe2+ with the CD, which was also supported by calculating the charge distribution (Fig. S5). Meanwhile, the CN stretching vibration at 1520 cm−1, which arised from the conjugation between benzene ring and nitrogen with lone pair electrons (pyridinic nitrogen), was weakened, while the stretching vibration peak of Fe–N appears at 400-600 cm−1 after Fe2+ adsorption [23,24]. These suggested the Fe2+ adsorption onto CD via the possible Fe–N chelation on pyridinic nitrogen of the framework. This Fe–N chelation brought the Fe2+ with high and irreversible oxidation-reduction potential, which thus could not catalyze the Fenton reaction due to the blocked reaction path (Fig. 2C). Investigation of ROS scavenging found that the surface groups of CD such as aminos contributed the reducing power. After reaction with ROS, these surface groups, especially the amino-N contained groups were obvious decreased as indicated by NMR and XPS (Fig. 2D and Fig. S6), which suggested their oxidation removal and conversion (such as conversion into CN species) after scavenging the ROS. This ROS scavenging destroyed the surface states and thus caused a decreased fluorescence emission especially under the exorbitant ROS (H2O2) stimulation (Herein, this didn't affect the fluorescence imaging of CD due to relatively low H2O2 concentration of about 100 μM) (Fig. 2E and Fig. S7). Moreover, the loss of surface N-contained groups enabled CD to profile a concentration-dependent NO release especially in the presence of oxidative microenvironment (Fig. 2F). And this process could be slightly inhibited after GSH addition. Thus, the NO release might be attributed to the nitrogen doping-introduced amino onto CD, enabling CD to release NO via oxidation reaction. These suggested the unique structure of CD for Fe2+ sequestration, ROS scavenging and NO release.

Next, DFT calculations validated the unique structure-function relationship. As shown by the electrostatic potential maps (Fig. 3 A–C), N-doping, especially the pyridine nitrogen doping, contributed to the electron-rich regions, which thus could provide lone pair electrons to facilitate the chelation of metal ions such as iron ions. Meanwhile, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy of as prepared CD with N-doping carbon core and surface functional groups, was calculated to be higher than those of pristine N-doping carbon dot (n-CD) and particularly the pure graphite carbon dot (g-CD) (Table S1), which suggested the electron donation capacity of CD [25]. Adsorption energies of Fe2+ on these nanoparticles is CD < n-CD < g-CD (Calculation according to the formula Eads = EAB-E(A)-E(B)) (Table S2), implying the easy adsorption of Fe2+ on CD. All these validated the as prepared CD with simultaneous N-doping and surface functional groups possessed the strongest ability to donate electrons, and thus capture the electron-deficient iron ions. After Fe2+ chelation onto CD to form CD-Fe2+, *OH would adsorb on it with high desorption energy of 2.16 eV (Table S2) during its catalytic decomposition of H2O2. This makes it extremely difficult for *OH to be detached from the CD-Fe2+, thus leading to the catalyst poisoning to inhibit Fenton reaction proceeding (Fig. 3D and E). Meanwhile, classical Fenton reaction usually involved the catalytic cycle from Fe2+ to Fe3+, and then back to Fe2+ (the rate-limiting step) [26], to maintain the catalytic activity for ROS generation. Nevertheless, CD adsorption (CD-Fe3+) shifted the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy of Fe3+ from −1.53 eV to −0.410 eV (Fig. 3F), which increased the difficulty in reduction of Fe3+ back to Fe2+ [27], and thus also contributed to the strong inhibition of Fenton reaction. This was in consistent with the results of the above electrochemical testing (Fig. 2C). Indicated by 1H NMR and XPS, the NO release might be attributed to the nitrogen doping-introduced amino onto CD since the amino-N contained groups were obviously decreased after reaction with ROS (Fig. 2D and Fig. S6). The results of DFT calculations suggested that the process might involve the oxidation of surface amino groups into hydroxylamine structures through two transition states according to results of Gibbs free energy variations (Fig. 3G and H). Furtherly, the resulting hydroxylamine groups can be further oxidized to produce NO [28]. All these confirmed the origination of Fe2+ sequestration, Fenton reaction prevention and ROS sacrifice from the unique N-doping coupling with surface groups of CD via this simple preparation.

Since CD possesses the virtues of red-emission, Fe2+ sequestration, ROS scavenging and NO release, it was expected to be a promised agent for antioxidative therapy of PD. To facilitate this application, polyethylene glycol (PEG) was modified onto CD via the covalent reaction between the –NH2 of CD and –NHS of PEG (Figs. S8 and 9), to improve the water dispersity (Fig. S10) and depress the high positive zeta potential of CD (Fig. 4 D). Simultaneously, Lf, known for its specific binding to the overexpressed Lf receptor on both the BBB and SN neurons [29], was attached to the PEGylated CD. Although PEGylation could decrease cell internalization of CD due to the shielded positive charge (Fig. 4E and F), it reduced the positive charge-induced cytotoxicity (Fig. S11). And the Lf modification could further improve these performances. Meanwhile, Lf modification brought an aggregate with average diameter of approximately 46.4 nm (Fig. S12), where CD with distinct lattice fringes was well-dispersedly observed (Fig. 4A and B). The formation of larger aggregate might be attributed to the cross-linking between CP and Lf, which was beneficial for the active targeting of CD (Figs. S13 and 14). Notably, these modifications do not alter the fluorescence properties of CD (Fig. 4C and Fig. S15). Accordingly, the as-obtained CPL possessed a selective high-uptake by brain microvascular endothelial cells (bEnd.3) and human neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y) as compared with that of normal hepatocytes (LO2), for obviously red fluorescence imaging (Fig. S16). This behavior was much superior to those of pristine CD and PEGylated CD, where the former had undifferentiated high uptake of CD or even the higher uptake by normal hepatocytes due to the positive charges, and the latter possessed a low uptake by all these cells due to absence of active targeting. Further investigation via the CD-enabled in vivo fluorescence imaging also found the high accumulation of CD both in brain and in other tissues of MPTP model (PD) mice (Fig. 4G–I). Comparatively, PEG modification prolonged the circulation of CD in the blood, thereby reducing their accumulation in these organs. Particularly, CPL possessed much higher accumulation into brain of PD mice, which was superior to CD and CP via the extra Lf-mediated targeting. Meanwhile, CPL had a higher accumulation and a longer retention in the brain of PD mice than those in the brain of normal mice (Fig. 4H). These might be attributed to the high-level oxidative stress and highly-expressed Lf receptor on the BBB of PD mice, which synergistically contributed the enhanced NO release and receptor-mediated transport for the BBB crossing and brain targeting. In addition, immunofluorescence images of brain sections also demonstrated the successful BBB crossing and accumulation of CPL nanoparticles in the brain (Fig. S17). Therefore, CPL could be served as an advanced brain-targeting agent for PD imaging.

Besides that, it was found the cells endocytosed CPL could exude from the cells with the prolonged co-incubation time, as demonstrated by the assay in Fig. 5 A. Herein, the intracellular fluorescence intensity gradually decreased and the extracellular solution's fluorescence intensity gradually increased via the prolonged incubation (Fig. 5B–D). Since the nanoparticles are positively charged and Lf-modified, there are probably two possible mechanisms of CPL escaping out of cells. One is exocytosis mediated by the electropositive surfaces of CPL, and the other is transcytosis mediated by Lf receptor [30,31]. Correspondingly, the exocytosed CPL and also the other counterparts in the supernatant contained the Fe species, demonstrating that the CD-based formulations could carry intracellular iron ions out of the cells (Fig. 5C). Notably, owing to the high cellular uptake of pristine positively charged CD, its ability to carry iron ions out of the cells was larger than that of CP. Also, CPL possessed an elevated Fe carrying ability despite that the modified Lf might contribute some Fe species. In cells, iron is typically bound within ferritin or other complexes, while a small fraction of iron exists in a labile, highly active form known as the labile iron pool (LIP) [32]. The LIP within cells could catalyze the ROS generation through Fenton reactions [33]. Therefore, the LIP was monitored by Calcein-AM fluorescence staining, where the change of fluorescence was corresponding to the LIP level. As shown in Fig. 5F, MPP+ model group possessed large LIP. However, via CD-involved nanoparticle treatment, the LIP was significantly reduced, in agreement with the Fe efflux performances of these nanoparticles. Correspondingly, cellular ROS was also depressed via especially the CPL treatment (Fig. 5E). All the behaviors were even outperformed the classical DFO. These validated that the CD-involved nanoparticles could chelate free irons out of cells, reduce the level of the LIP within cells, and subsequently achieve a reduction in cellular ROS, thereby contributing to an increase in the survival rate of MPP+-induced PD model cells (Fig. 5G). Noteworthily, pure Lf treatment could also reduce the LIP and ROS in MPP+-treated cells to contribute increased cell viability. This may be attributed to that Lf can reduce the expression of DMT1 (a key protein for iron transport) to inhibit the reflux of extracellular iron ions into the cells (Fig. 5H) [34]. Therefore, the CPL could synergistically sequestrate intracellular Fe2+ and simultaneously depress the intracellular ROS to reduce the ferroptosis and ROS damage of neurocytes.

Moreover, CD could generate NO within SH-SY5Y cells, which also demonstrated a concentration-dependent manner in consistent with the findings in solution (Fig. 5I). As one of the most important second messengers in cells, NO plays essential roles in the modulation of various biological functions [35]. NO can improve the activity of MMP-9 by inducing S-nitrosylation of cysteine groups on it, further regulating the expression of tight junction protein [36]. Therefore, CPL's impact on the expression of tight junction relevant proteins was investigated in the BBB model, where a significant decrease in ZO-1 and claudin-5 expression levels accompanied by an increase in MMP-9 expression were observed via the CPL treatment (Fig. 5J and Fig. S18). This implied the NO-induced BBB opening via modulating these BBB-relative protein expressions. Interestingly, the expressions of these proteins were recovered at 24 h after removal of CPL, indicating that the BBB opening induced by CPL is temporary and reversible (Fig. 5J). This was beneficial for brain targeting drug delivery since the temporary opening and recovery could enhance the BBB crossing and decrease the side effects. Herein, CPL contributed the optimal performances with more effective BBB transport across in vitro BBB model than the other counterparts due to the simultaneous Lf-mediated BBB targeting (Fig. 5K). These results clearly showed that CPL enhanced transport across the BBB, leading to increased intracephalic uptake and ensuring neuroprotection. This effect was achieved through the removal of intracellular iron ions, inhibition of reflux, and scavenging of ROS with reduced generation. In addition, the CD-based formulations may also protect cells in other cellular pathways by NO [37,38].

Therefore, in vivo neuroprotective therapy of CPL was comparatively investigated by behavior tests on MPTP-induced PD mice (Fig. 6 A). Compared to normal mice, MPTP-treated mice remained on the rotating rod for significantly shorter periods due to PD-related brain damage. However, after CPL treatment, these mice showed a marked improvement, staying on the rod much longer than untreated PD mice, indicating a significant enhancement in their PD-affected behavior (Fig. 6B). Consistent with in vitro results, CD and Lf also slightly improved the number of turns for PD mice on the rotating rod, though to a lesser extent than the CPL group. Accordingly, the total travelled distance, average speed and central motion ratio (middle/all) for the PD mice were obviously increased especially after CPL treatment (Fig. 6C–F). These results suggested that CPL was able to alleviate the symptoms of PD mice, whose efficacy was superior to those of the other counterparts such as free CD or Lf. Particularly, it was found that the combined treatments via CPL and levodopa (LDA) could even rescue the PD mice nearly to the normal standard, which was much superior to classical LDA treatment. This suggested the synergistical therapeutic outcomes addressed both the symptoms and root cause, where CPL could fundamentally protect neurons and LDA could remedy neurotransmitter. These further demonstrated that CPL is an advanced neuroprotective agent.

To validate the neuroprotective functions of CPL, neurotransmitters were detected on MPTP-induced PD mice after different treatments. Tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) is crucial for dopamine (DA) biosynthesis and reflects dopaminergic neuron health [39], with TH level decreases indicating dopaminergic neuronal loss [40]. Fig. 7 A showed a notable reduction in TH-positive neurons in the striatum and SN following MPTP administration, highlighting dopaminergic neuron damage. However, CPL treatment significantly reversed this effect more effectively than other treatments. Given DA's role in PD, DA concentrations in the SN were measured using LC-MS/MS (Fig. 7B). The DA levels were significantly higher in the CPL-treated mice compared to the MPTP group, underscoring CPL's potent therapeutic impact. To further elucidate this point, the intracerebral iron deposition was determined by immunohistochemistry and ICP (Fig. 7C and D). Significant iron accumulation happened in the brain after MPTP treatment. Whereas, intravenous administration of CPL caused obvious iron reduction in the SN, which was much more obvious than those for other groups. These validated the efficient relieving of iron deposition in the brain of PD mice via CPL treatment, which was accounted for the reduced dopaminergic neuron death and restoring the behavior deficits. Herein, Lf also possesses some effects in relieving iron deposition. This may be attributed to the reduced DMT1 expression via Lf treatment according to previous reports [34,41], which acted as a key protein for iron transport to prevent the Fe reflux into the cells (Fig. S19).

Reducing iron deposition and scavenging ROS could improve the inflammatory environment, which thus can contribute to microglia normalization [42]. To examine this point, the polarization of microglia was detected by staining the brain slices from the mice after different treatments (Fig. 7E and Fig. S20). Mice treated with CPL exhibited a higher number of M2-like microglial cells compared to other treatment groups. Additionally, these mice showed fewer M1-like microglia than those in the PD group. Furthermore, malondialdehyde (MDA) levels confirmed that CPL treatment notably reduced oxidative stress (Fig. 7F). These results emphasize CPL's efficacy in converting proinflammatory M1-like microglia to anti-inflammatory M2-like microglia in treating Parkinson's disease, by decreasing iron accumulation and reactive oxygen species in the brain. Correspondingly, neurotoxic IL-1β, IL-6 and typical pro-inflammatory cytokine (TNF-α) in PD mice were also obviously downregulated via CPL treatment, confirming the superiority of CPL in synergistical reduction of oxidative stress against PD disease (Fig. 7G–I). To evaluate the biosafety of the CD-based nanoparticles, major organs from treated mice were examined via hematoxylin/eosin (H&E) staining (Fig. S21). The results showed no detectable pathological changes in these organs or in their serum biochemical indicators (Fig. S22). Meanwhile, no obvious hemolysis occurred after 4 h of co-incubation with different treatments (Fig. S23). Especially, the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of the rats after treatment for 2 h were collected for fluorescence measurements (Fig. S24). And it was found that CPL had the highest accumulation in the CSF. Given the crucial role of CSF in clearing metabolites from the brain, this intriguing finding not only confirms CPL's enhanced ability to cross the BBB and enter the brain, but also suggests the potential for CPL to be cleared through CSF circulation. Furthermore, this also provides a potential pathway for CPL to carry irons away from the brain. Therefore, CPL is a synergistic antioxidation and BBB-crossing agent with potential biosafety for PD treatment.

In summary, red-emitting N-doping CD was facilely synthesized by a solvothermal reaction of p-phenylenediamines. Attributing to the plentiful surface electron-rich groups of amino conjugated to the sp2-hybrided π-system of carbon cores, this CD exhibited large reduction potential, which thus could sacrifice ROS and produce NO especially under the oxidative stress stimulation. Attributing to the pyridine N-doping-induced electron localization, this CD could chelate the free iron ions and thus depress the catalytic Fe cycle and *OH detaching to inhibit the Fenton reaction. After being modified with Lf, the resulting CPL can cross the BBB nondestructively through both Lf receptor-mediated transport and NO-mediated reversible opening of the BBB. Meanwhile, it could target and accumulate in the dopaminergic neurons due to the Lf receptor mediation, where the iron sequestration and ROS sacrificing were achieved via the special chelation and reduction effects of CD, and synergizing with the iron reflux prevention of Lf. Consequently, this CPL could image the PD, reduce the oxidative stress and inflammatory reaction in the brain, and improve the behavioral performance of PD mice in MPTP models. In addition, CPL could be metabolized out of brain via the CSF circulation, which wouldn't cause obvious side effects. This work promises an alternative agent for PD treatment and a comprehensive understanding for its functions.

Wei Guo: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Min Qian: Software, Project administration, Methodology. Yanhui Qin: Methodology. Wenshuai Li: Software, Methodology, Conceptualization. Huifang Nie: Software, Methodology. Wenxin Lv: Software, Methodology. Guangwei Jiang: Methodology. Rong Huang: Methodology. Chenteng Lin: Methodology. Hongyuan Li: Methodology. Rongqin Huang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition.
